query: cause of delirium tremens in alcoholics

retrieved_docs.title: Delirium Tremens(Archived) -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: The 12-month and lifetime prevalence is highest in adult men, with 17.6% and 36% respectively. There is a higher prevalence in the White, younger population and in those who were never married or previously married. The lifetime risk for developing DT in the population with alcohol use disorder is approximately 5% to 10%. Risk factors for DT include the following: a history of DT prior history of seizures the presence of concurrent illness with associated comorbidities prior history of detoxification the prolonged period prior to the last drink. [5] [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Delirium Tremens(Archived) -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Alcohol use disorder is a common condition that has been associated with severe impairments in social functioning and medical problems. As high as 20% of the population have been noted to exhibit alcohol overuse during their lifespan. More than 50% of those with a history of alcohol use disorder can exhibit alcohol withdrawal symptoms at discontinuing or decreasing their alcohol use. However, only a few (3% to 5%) exhibit symptoms of severe alcohol withdrawal with profound confusion, autonomic hyperactivity, and cardiovascular collapse. This is defined as alcohol withdrawal delirium, more commonly known as delirium tremens (DT).

retrieved_docs.title: Withdrawal Syndromes -- Epidemiology -- Alcohol
retrieved_docs.content: According to data from the National Epidemiologic Survey on alcohol and related conditions, the estimated lifetime prevalence of alcohol use disorder was 12.8%. [22] Approximately 20% of adults in the emergency room may have alcohol use disorder, and about 4% to 40% of patients admitted to the intensive care unit will have alcohol withdrawal symptoms (AWS). [23] [24] Patients hospitalized with AWS have an increased length of hospital stay and increased mortality compared to patients without AWS. [25] Chronic alcoholism and withdrawal are more common in men than in women. [23] The mortality rate from alcohol withdrawal and delirium tremens is high if untreated, with as many as 5% of these patients developing delirium tremens when they withdraw from chronic alcohol use.

retrieved_docs.title: Delirium Tremens(Archived) -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Delirium tremens occurs in chronic alcohol users who abruptly discontinue alcohol use, often as early as 48 hours. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Delirium Tremens(Archived) -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Delirium tremens was first recognized as a disorder attributed to excessive alcohol use in 1813. It is now commonly known to occur as early as 48 hours after abrupt cessation of alcohol in those with chronic use and can last up to 5 days. It has an anticipated mortality of up to 37% without appropriate treatment. It is crucial to identify early signs of withdrawal because it can become fatal. [1] [2] [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Delirium Tremens(Archived) -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Recognizing individuals with a history of alcohol use disorder can help prevent the progression of withdrawal symptoms. The US Preventative Services Task Force recommends screening individuals aged 18 or older involved with risky drinking and engaging these individuals with behavior therapy and interventions to decrease alcohol misuse. There is a lack of consensus on the prophylactic treatment of alcohol withdrawal. Variations in hospital-wide policies in treating alcohol withdrawal exist, and the medications used include benzodiazepines and even gabapentin.

retrieved_docs.title: Delirium Tremens(Archived) -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: The duration since the last drink becomes critical in recognizing the severity of symptoms. The initial minor withdrawal symptoms are characterized by anxiety, insomnia, palpitations, headache, and gastrointestinal symptoms. These symptoms usually occur as early as 6 hours after cessation of alcohol use. After 12 hours, minor withdrawal symptoms can progress to alcohol hallucinosis, a condition characterized by visual hallucinations. It can typically resolve in 24 to 48 hours and may also be associated with auditory and tactile hallucinations. Alcohol withdrawal seizure is followed by alcohol hallucinosis, though it may present as early as 6 hours before alcohol hallucinosis. It can present as the only withdrawal symptoms but typically resolves 24 to 48 hours later. Seizures can recur, though rarely lead to status epilepticus. Uncharacteristic signs of seizure activity should warrant further workup. If withdrawal symptoms remain untreated, this can typically lead to DT. DT is characterized by visual hallucinations, profound confusion, tachycardia, hypertension, hyperthermia, agitation, and diaphoresis.

retrieved_docs.title: Alcohol Withdrawal Syndrome -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Alcohol withdrawal delirium, formerly known as delirium tremens, is the most severe manifestation of alcohol withdrawal syndrome, characterized by symptoms such as fever, tachycardia, agitation, diaphoresis, hallucinations, disorientation, and hypertension. Patients are at risk of experiencing alcohol withdrawal delirium anywhere from 3 to 8 days following alcohol cessation. While only approximately 3% to 5% of patients with alcohol withdrawal syndrome will progress to alcohol withdrawal delirium, this condition may prove fatal. [2] Identifying patients at risk for alcohol withdrawal delirium through a validated tool and considering the patient’s history of complicated withdrawal is a crucial aspect of managing the syndrome. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Delirium Tremens(Archived) -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Alcohol also inhibits the action of NMDA receptors by acting as a receptor antagonist. It inhibits the action of glutamate, which is an excitatory amino acid. Prolonged alcohol use results in receptor up-regulation. Abrupt discontinuation of alcohol causes an increase in the action of glutamate, resulting in profound excitatory action. This may have a clinical manifestation of sympathetic overdrive, such as agitation, tremors, tachycardia, and hypertension.

retrieved_docs.title: Delirium Tremens(Archived) -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Clinicians need to evaluate the severity of alcohol withdrawal based on history and clinical presentation. The best-validated tool to assess the severity of alcohol withdrawal is the Clinical Institute Withdrawal Assessment for Alcohol, Revised (CIWA-Ar). It is a 10-item questionnaire tool to evaluate, monitor, and treat alcohol withdrawal. It includes symptoms of withdrawal such as anxiety, nausea, and sweating, among others. A score of 8 points or lower corresponds to mild withdrawal, while a score of 9 to 15 corresponds to moderate withdrawal, and a score of 15 or greater corresponds to severe withdrawal symptoms, being at risk for seizures and DT. [9] [10] CIWA-Ar is not recommended for withdrawal delirium due to its subjective nature and patients' inability to accurately report withdrawal symptoms.

retrieved_docs.title: Withdrawal Syndromes -- History and Physical -- Alcohol
retrieved_docs.content: The alcohol withdrawal syndrome is a spectrum and ranges from anxiety and mild tremors to potentially life-threatening delirium tremens characterized by autonomic hyperactivity, tachypnea, hyperthermia, and diaphoresis. Symptoms of insomnia, anxiety, and mild tremors can occur while there is still a detectable alcohol level in the patient's blood work. Alcoholic hallucinosis is characterized by visual and tactile hallucinations, with an otherwise clear sensorium, and one can understand that the hallucinations are not real. Due to the kindling effect of alcohol withdrawal, more severe and progressive symptoms occur with subsequent withdrawal episodes.

retrieved_docs.title: Withdrawal Syndromes -- Treatment / Management -- Alcohol
retrieved_docs.content: Patients undergoing alcohol withdrawal may have numerous potentially life-threatening medical problems, which should be identified and treated along with their withdrawal. The administration of intravenous glucose empiricly to patients with seizures is controversial because this is thought to precipitate acute Wernicke encephalopathy in chronic alcoholism unless thiamine is also administered. If the patient is hypoglycemic, glucose should not be withheld, and dextrose 50% in water (25 mL to 50 mL) and thiamine (100 mg intravenously) should be provided. Although low doses of clonidine can help reverse central adrenergic discharge, relieving tachypnea, tachycardia, hypertension, tremor, and craving for alcohol, it has a minimal role in the treatment of acute alcohol withdrawal and should never be used as monotherapy. [55] The drugs of choice for the treatment of alcohol withdrawal in the inpatient setting are benzodiazepines. Due to its preferable pharmacokinetics, Diazepam is the benzodiazepine of choice, especially with a history of prior delirium tremens and severe withdrawal symptoms. [56] Lorazepam may be preferable in patients with preexisting liver disease.

retrieved_docs.title: Withdrawal Syndromes -- History and Physical -- Alcohol
retrieved_docs.content: Seizures are often seen in those who have had multiple past episodes of withdrawal and attempts to detoxify. An alternative diagnosis should be considered if a seizure occurs in the absence of an alcohol use history, there is evidence of trauma or fever, the seizure is focal, or the patient's last drink was over 48 hours prior. Alcohol withdrawal delirium, commonly known as delirium tremens, is life-threatening and carries a mortality risk of 1% to 5%. [37] Risk factors for the development of delirium tremens include a coexisting medical illness, heavy and prolonged alcohol use, previous history of severe withdrawal, older age, and abnormal life function testing. [38] [39]

retrieved_docs.title: Delirium Tremens(Archived) -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Alcohol acts as a central nervous system depressant. It enhances the effect of inhibitory neurotransmitters while down-regulating excitatory neurotransmitters. Alcohol interacts with GABA receptors, chloride ion receptor acting as an inhibitory neurotransmitter, via several mechanisms to enhance its activity. Over time, through prolonged alcohol exposure, there is a decrease in GABA activity and alteration in the type of GABA receptor and function. Abrupt cessation of alcohol causes a decrease in the inhibitory actions of the GABA neurotransmitter, resulting in overactivity of the central nervous system.

retrieved_docs.title: Withdrawal Syndromes -- Complications -- Alcohol
retrieved_docs.content: Complications of alcohol withdrawal include anxiety, depression, sleep disturbances, and hallucinations with disturbances of perception. More serious complications include seizures, delirium tremens, Wernicke-Korsakoff syndrome, and death. Although not a true complication of withdrawal, the Wernicke-Korsakoff syndrome is a nutritional deficiency of thiamine that often coincides with alcohol use and withdrawal; this syndrome is characterized by confusion with delirium, gait disturbances with ataxia, and paralysis of the eye muscles, termed ophthalmoplegia. Long-term memory impairment can occur in up to 80% of patients with alcohol use disorder who experience this syndrome. [71] [72] A protracted withdrawal syndrome can occur, with symptoms of anhedonia, irritability, insomnia, and craving, which occur past the expected course of standard withdrawal and potentially can be a reason for relapse. [73]

retrieved_docs.title: Delirium Tremens(Archived) -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: The treatment goals for alcohol withdrawal are to control agitation, decrease the risk of seizures, and decrease morbidity and mortality. The most common and validated treatment for alcohol withdrawal is benzodiazepine. Several benzodiazepines can be used, including lorazepam, diazepam, and chlordiazepoxide, preferably administered via an intravenous route. This can be administered based on a symptom-triggered regimen or a fixed schedule. In a symptom-triggered regimen, medications are usually given when symptoms are present, sometimes using a CIWA score greater than 8. In a fixed schedule regimen, the benzodiazepine is administered at fixed intervals, and additional doses are given based on the withdrawal symptoms.

Within the psychosis SMQ, mood‑related diagnoses such as recurrent and single‑episode major depressive episodes, melancholic depression (اكتئاب أَوبِيّ), mood lability, and mania (هوس شَبَقِيّ) are also members, highlighting overlap among depressive, manic and psychotic features. The non‑infectious encephalitis SMQ includes the two awakening‑related disorders, indicating that arousal disturbances may manifest as encephalitic processes. Non‑infectious encephalitis is characterized by hyperkinetic motor activity, increased muscular activity and pronounced early‑phase excessive daytime sleepiness. In the broader non‑infectious encephalopathy spectrum, apathy (اللامبالاة) is a component of the delirium/brain‑dysfunction syndrome (الهذيان/الاعتلال الدماغي غير المعدي (SMQ)), which also encompasses attention deficits, reduced

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 1882
retrieved_docs.content: Alcohol-related disorders are linked to several therapeutic and clinical considerations. Oxazepam and clorazepate are identified as medications that may be used to treat alcohol-related disorders, reflecting the role of benzodiazepines in managing withdrawal and related symptoms. Clinically, alcohol-related disorders are associated with حالة ارتباكية, indicating a frequent presentation of confusion or altered mental status in affected patients. The condition also has a noted relationship with الهَلْوَسة, suggesting an additional clinical or cultural manifestation tied to these disorders. Importantly, beta-D-glucopyranose is contraindicated in the context of alcohol-related disorders, highlighting that this compound should be avoided in patients with this condition to prevent adverse effects. Together, these connections underscore the importance of appropriate pharmacologic selection, awareness of neurocognitive complications, and avoidance of contraindicated substances in the care of individuals with alcohol-related disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2270
retrieved_docs.content: Organic mental disorder caused by psychoactive substance is a broad diagnostic category that includes several specific subtypes. Among these, Sedative, hypnotic AND/OR anxiolytic intoxication delirium represents a form of delirium that arises from the acute intoxication of depressant substances such as sedatives, hypnotics, or anxiolytics. Another distinct subtype is intoxicatie; hallucinogeen, which denotes mental disorder manifestations resulting from the acute intoxication of hallucinogenic agents. Recognizing that both Sedative, hypnotic AND/OR anxiolytic intoxication delirium and intoxicatie; hallucinogeen are child categories of Organic mental disorder caused by psychoactive substance helps clinicians differentiate the underlying substance class, anticipate characteristic clinical features, and tailor appropriate treatment and management strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1864
retrieved_docs.content: Alcohol-related disorders are clinically associated with a confusional state (حالة ارتباكية) and are also linked to hallucinations (الهَلْوَسة). Management of alcohol-related disorders may include the use of benzodiazepine agents such as clorazepate and prazepam, which are considered potential treatments for the symptoms and complications of these disorders.

Alcohol‑induced disorders are classified as a subtype of substance‑use disorder, confirming their inclusion in the broader substance‑use category. Non‑infectious encephalitis (SMQ) is associated with hyperkinetic manifestations such as motor hyperactivity, muscular hyperactivity, and primary excessive daytime sleepiness. The non‑infectious delirium/brain‑dysfunction syndrome (الهذيان/الاعتلال الدماغي غير المعدي (SMQ)) is defined by weak concentration and excessive daytime sleepiness, while organic psychosis (ذُهان عضوي) is linked to manic psychosis (هوس شَبَقِيّ), indicating that psychotic‑like delusions or hallucinations may arise in medically based mental disorders. Together, these hierarchical and intersecting categories provide clinicians with an integrated framework for recognizing overlapping symptom clusters, differentiating primary psychiatric conditions from organic mental disorders, and guiding comprehensive assessment and treatment planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 1617
retrieved_docs.content: Alcohol-related disorders are clinically associated with حالة ارتباكية, indicating that patients with these disorders often experience confusional states. They also have a relationship with الهَلْوَسة, reflecting a frequent occurrence of hallucinations in this population. Clorazepate may be used to treat alcohol-related disorders, offering a pharmacologic option to address the neuropsychiatric symptoms such as confusion and hallucinations that commonly accompany excessive alcohol use. This interconnected knowledge underscores the importance of recognizing both the mental health manifestations and appropriate medication strategies in the care of individuals with alcohol-related disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2217
retrieved_docs.content: Psychoactive substances are the causative agents of psychoactive substance withdrawal, meaning that when the use of a psychoactive substance is reduced or stopped, individuals may experience a withdrawal syndrome characterized by a range of physical and psychological symptoms. Recognizing that psychoactive substances precipitate withdrawal is essential for clinicians to anticipate, prevent, and manage these symptoms, thereby improving patient safety and supporting successful cessation or tapering strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 3 -- Community 2283
retrieved_docs.content: Alcohol-related disorders are closely linked to several clinical manifestations and diagnostic findings. They are clinically associated with تَشَمَّعٌ كُحولِيّ (alcoholic intoxication) and حالة ارتباكيّة (confusional state), indicating that patients with these disorders often present with acute intoxication symptoms and altered mental status. In addition, an abnormal chest X‑ray (الأشعة السينية على الصَدْر غير طبيعية) is also clinically associated with تَشَمَّعٌ كُحولِيّ, suggesting that respiratory or pulmonary abnormalities may be observed in the context of severe alcohol intoxication. Alcohol-related disorders also have a relationship with الهَلْوَسة, further emphasizing the neuropsychiatric complications that can accompany excessive alcohol use. Pharmacologically, clorazepate and prazepam are identified as agents that may be used to treat alcohol-related disorders, providing therapeutic options for managing withdrawal, anxiety, or other related symptoms. Recognizing these interconnections aids clinicians in comprehensive assessment and targeted management of patients with alcohol-related conditions.

Delirium/non‑inflammatory brain pathology includes apathy (اللامبالاة), attention disorder, concentration weakness, and excessive daytime sleepiness. The term ذُهان عضوي describes mental disorders with an organic or biological basis and subsumes a broader class of organic psychotic disorders. Within this class, هوس شَبَقِيّ is identified as a specific type of delusional mania that exhibits psychotic‑like features and is classified as an organic psychotic disorder. These hierarchical relationships illustrate how mental‑symptom domains intersect with organic psychotic conditions, how diverse depressive phenotypes are organized under a unified depression framework, and how substance‑use, behavior, psychotic, and neuro‑behavioral manifestations are integrated to support comprehensive clinical assessment and targeted patient care.

The syndrome itself is classified under خلل في الانتباه (attention disorder) and under ضعف التركيز (concentration impairment). Thus, attention deficits and reduced concentration are core aspects of delirium/non‑organic brain syndrome, and apathy may appear as one of its manifestations. Recognizing these interrelated signs supports more accurate assessment and management of patients with cognitive and attentional disturbances.
